notes.anchor: by every order, with the votes in each order falling all one way
notes.note: A bribery jury was drawn in thirds from senators, knights, and tribuni aerarii (the three 'orders'); Caelius's point is that the scandalous acquittal carried unanimously in all three panels.

notes.anchor: he comes up under the Lex Licinia
notes.note: Crassus's Lex Licinia de sodaliciis of 55 BC, against the electoral clubs (sodalicia) used to organise bribery; a man acquitted on the general bribery charge could be retried under this stricter law, hence the defendant's worse danger.

C. Scribonius Curio had built a famous double revolving wooden theatre for his father's funeral games; Hortensius, the great advocate who had secured the unpopular acquittal, was hissed there by an outraged crowd.
The image is of theatre rigging: the hissing of the audience is likened to the whistling of a ship's rope-ends in a gale, part of the storm-of-noise metaphor Caelius piles up.

notes.anchor: the moment you hear of my being elected designate
notes.note: Caelius was standing for the aedileship of 50 BC; an 'aedile designate' was one elected but not yet entered on office. The aedile's chief public duty was staging the games, which is why he immediately raises the panthers.
As aedile Caelius would stage games and wanted exotic beasts; he repeatedly badgers Cicero, as governor of Cilicia near beast-rich Asia, to procure and ship him panthers.
The commentarium rerum urbanarum, the running digest of Roman political and social news that Caelius had commissioned (see 8.1) and forwarded to Cicero in instalments by successive couriers.
